steps.title: Find the Key Signal
steps.content: The passage contrasts what individual studies emphasize (“population density,” “average commuting distance,” etc.) with the broader reality that “walking decisions are made in complex contexts in which multiple conditions and needs inform individuals' choices.” The “but” signals these factors are recognized by studies, but insufficient on their own.

steps.title: Apply the Signal to the Choices
steps.content:
D correctly frames these factors as “identified as important influences” but only “some of the many factors”—exactly the passage's logic.

A and C mischaracterize or reverse the passage's points about these factors, while B wrongly confines them to Copenhagen.

That matches Choice D — They represent factors that have been identified as important influences on walking decisions but that the text suggests are merely some of the many factors that may contribute to people's decision-making about walking..
